pages.title: AGENTS
pages.body:
# Documentation Workflow

- Use `Play-Git-Docs-Internal/` as the source of truth when creating or updating external documentation.
- Treat internal documentation as reference material only: do not expose internal-only implementation details, private notes, credentials, unreleased plans, or operational procedures unless explicitly requested.
- Prefer rewriting internal material for an external audience instead of copying it verbatim.
- When facts conflict, trust internal documentation first and call out uncertainty before changing external docs.

pages.title: Google OAuth
pages.description: Google-based sign-in for invited and existing users.

frontmatter.title: Google OAuth
frontmatter.description: Google-based sign-in for invited and existing users.

pages.body:
Google OAuth is the social sign-in option for Playtagon users.

## MVP Rules

- The Google account email must match the expected user identity for invite-based entry.
- Existing users can use Google OAuth as a returning sign-in method.
- If the user belongs to several studios, Playtagon shows [[Auth Multi Studio]].
- If the user has no access yet, Playtagon routes them to [[Auth Waitlist]].

pages.title: Invite Email
pages.description: Email-bound invitation flow for joining a studio.

frontmatter.title: Invite Email
frontmatter.description: Email-bound invitation flow for joining a studio.

pages.body:
Invite Email is used when a studio invites a specific person. The invitation is bound to the recipient email and opens a token-based entry flow.

## Flow

1. An owner or permitted member sends an invitation from [[Members Invite]].
2. The recipient opens the email link.
3. Playtagon verifies the token and email.
4. The user signs in or confirms identity.
5. The user lands in the invited studio with the assigned access.

Invite Email is the safest MVP path for named collaborators because permissions are prepared before the user joins.

pages.title: Invite Link
pages.description: Reusable studio invite link with default access settings.

frontmatter.title: Invite Link
frontmatter.description: Reusable studio invite link with default access settings.

pages.body:
Invite Link lets a studio invite collaborators through a shared link. It is useful when the exact user list is not final yet, but the studio still wants a controlled default access level.

## MVP Behavior

- The link is created from [[Members Invite]].
- The studio defines default access before sharing it.
- A reset invalidates the previous link.
- The joining user still needs to authenticate through [[Auth Magic Link]] or [[Auth Google OAuth]].

For sensitive projects, prefer [[Auth Invite Email]] because it is bound to a known email address.

pages.title: Magic Link
pages.description: Passwordless email sign-in for Playtagon users.

frontmatter.title: Magic Link
frontmatter.description: Passwordless email sign-in for Playtagon users.

pages.body:
Magic Link is the email-based sign-in method for users who do not use Google OAuth.

## Flow

1. The user enters an email address.
2. Playtagon sends a time-limited sign-in link.
3. The user opens the link in the same browser or device.
4. Playtagon resolves the user's access state and sends them to the correct next screen.

Magic Link can complete an invite flow or return an existing user to their studio.

pages.title: Multi-Studio Selection
pages.description: Workspace switcher for users who belong to more than one studio.

frontmatter.title: Multi-Studio Selection
frontmatter.description: Workspace switcher for users who belong to more than one studio.

pages.body:
Multi-Studio Selection appears after sign-in when a user has access to more than one studio.

## What It Shows

- Studio name and logo.
- The user's role or access summary.
- The most recent workspace when available.

After selection, the user lands in [[Games]] for the chosen studio. The active studio controls which games, members and settings are visible.

pages.title: Waitlist
pages.description: Access request path for users who are not yet approved.

frontmatter.title: Waitlist
frontmatter.description: Access request path for users who are not yet approved.

pages.body:
Waitlist is the entry path for users who do not have an invite or existing studio access.

## MVP Data

The form should collect enough information to qualify access without exposing internal approval operations:

- Name and work email.
- Studio or company name.
- Intended use of Playtagon.
- Optional note for the Playtagon team.

After submission, the user waits for approval or an invitation. The approval workflow itself is internal and not part of the external MVP documentation.

pages.title: Auth
pages.description: Entry points for approved users, invited users and waitlist applicants.

frontmatter.title: Auth
frontmatter.description: Entry points for approved users, invited users and waitlist applicants.

pages.body:
Auth is the entry layer for Playtagon. In MVP, a user either joins through an invitation, signs in with a magic link or Google OAuth, or applies through the waitlist.

## Supported Paths

| Path | Use case |
| --- | --- |
| [[Auth Invite Email]] | A studio owner invites a specific email address. |
| [[Auth Invite Link]] | A studio shares a reusable invite link. |
| [[Auth Magic Link]] | The user signs in by email without a password. |
| [[Auth Google OAuth]] | The user signs in with a Google account. |
| [[Auth Waitlist]] | A new user requests access before approval. |
| [[Auth Multi Studio]] | A user with several studios chooses the active workspace. |

Registration and sign-in are intentionally part of one flow: Playtagon decides the next screen from the user's access state.

pages.title: Games Dashboard
pages.description: Studio portfolio view for creating and tracking MVP games.

frontmatter.title: Games Dashboard
frontmatter.description: Studio portfolio view for creating and tracking MVP games.

pages.body:
Games Dashboard is the first working screen inside a studio. It lists the studio's games and gives users the entry point for creating a new Play SDK game.

## MVP Actions

- Create a new game with [[Types Play SDK]].
- Open an existing [[Game]].
- See the current publishing status.
- See the latest RGS or publishing check result when available.
- Switch between compact and detailed portfolio views.

## Game Statuses

| Status | Meaning |
| --- | --- |
| Collect Info | The game is being prepared for submission. |
| On Review | A submission is being reviewed. |
| Review Pass / Review Fail | Review has completed with a result. |
| 48h Period | The approved version is waiting before deployment. |
| Game Deployment | The game is being deployed. |
| Live | The game is live through RGS. |

Only the Play SDK creator type is active in MVP.

pages.title: Game Access
pages.description: Per-game access view for studio members.

frontmatter.title: Game Access
frontmatter.description: Per-game access view for studio members.

pages.body:
Game Access shows which games a member can open and what they can do inside each one.

## MVP Behavior

- Access can differ by game.
- A member can have edit access in one game and view-only access in another.
- Mixed access should be visible at the studio level so owners can understand the member's effective permissions.

The game-side view of the same data is [[Game Settings Members]].

pages.title: Invite Members
pages.description: Invite collaborators by email or invite link.

frontmatter.title: Invite Members
frontmatter.description: Invite collaborators by email or invite link.

pages.body:
Members Invite is the MVP workflow for adding people to a studio.

## Invite Methods

- Email invite: send access to a specific email address through [[Auth Invite Email]].
- Invite link: share a reusable link configured with default access through [[Auth Invite Link]].

## Setup

Before sending an invite, choose the member's role, role template and game access. This ensures the user lands in the studio with the right permissions already applied.

pages.title: Member Permissions
pages.description: Permission model for studio, game and module access.

frontmatter.title: Member Permissions
frontmatter.description: Permission model for studio, game and module access.

pages.body:
Permissions define what a member can do after they join a studio.

## Levels

- Studio-level permissions control broad studio operations.
- Game-level permissions control access to a specific game.
- Module-level permissions control tools such as [[Resources]], [[Launch Game]], [[Promo Assets]] and [[Publishing]].

## States

| State | Meaning |
| --- | --- |
| Denied | The area is not visible or usable. |
| View | The member can read information. |
| Edit | The member can change information or perform actions. |

Permissions are explicit after setup. Role templates only provide a starting point.

pages.title: Remove Members
pages.description: Revoke a member’s studio and game access.

frontmatter.title: Remove Members
frontmatter.description: Revoke a member’s studio and game access.

pages.body:
Remove Members is used when a collaborator should no longer have access to a studio.

## MVP Behavior

- Removing a member revokes studio access.
- Game and module permissions are removed with the member.
- Active sessions should no longer grant access after removal.
- Historical references in publishing or review records should remain readable as past activity.

Use [[Members Sessions]] when the immediate concern is active device access.

pages.title: Role Templates
pages.description: Starting permission presets for common studio collaborators.

frontmatter.title: Role Templates
frontmatter.description: Starting permission presets for common studio collaborators.

pages.body:
Role Templates are presets that help owners grant access quickly. After applying a template, permissions can be edited manually.

## MVP Templates

| Template | Typical access |
| --- | --- |
| Game Lead | Broad game access, publishing coordination and team visibility. |
| Developer | Resources, Launch Game, SDK/RGS checks and technical publishing fields. |
| QA Tester | Launch Game, replays and review-related information. |
| Analyst | Insights and read access to game status. |
| Marketing Manager | Promo Assets and public game metadata. |
| Viewer | Read-only access where safe. |

Templates are not separate roles. They are shortcuts for [[Members Permissions]].

pages.title: Member Roles
pages.description: Base roles used for studio access.

frontmatter.title: Member Roles
frontmatter.description: Base roles used for studio access.

pages.body:
Playtagon MVP uses two base roles at studio level.

| Role | Access model |
| --- | --- |
| Owner | Full control over the studio, members, settings and critical actions. |
| Member | Access is controlled by explicit permissions and role templates. |

Viewer is not a base role. It is a [[Members Role Templates|role template]] that starts with read-only permissions.

pages.title: Member Sessions
pages.description: View and revoke active member sessions.

frontmatter.title: Member Sessions
frontmatter.description: View and revoke active member sessions.

pages.body:
Member Sessions shows active sessions for studio members when the current user has permission to manage access.

## MVP Use Cases

- Check whether a member is currently signed in.
- Revoke sessions after role or permission changes.
- Revoke sessions when a device is lost or access should end immediately.

Users can manage their own sessions from [[Profile Sessions]].

pages.title: Personal Data
pages.description: User identity settings for the Playtagon account.

frontmatter.title: Personal Data
frontmatter.description: User identity settings for the Playtagon account.

pages.body:
Personal Data stores the user's visible account details.

## MVP Fields

- Avatar.
- Display name.
- Preferred language.
- Timezone.

These settings follow the user across studios. They do not change studio membership or permissions.

pages.title: Profile Sessions
pages.description: Personal active-session management.

frontmatter.title: Profile Sessions
frontmatter.description: Personal active-session management.

pages.body:
Profile Sessions lets a user review and revoke their own active sessions.

## MVP Behavior

- Show current and recent sessions.
- Identify device, browser and approximate location when available.
- Let the user sign out of another session.

Studio owners can manage member sessions from [[Members Sessions]] when needed.

pages.title: Profile
pages.description: Personal account area for identity and sessions.

frontmatter.title: Profile
frontmatter.description: Personal account area for identity and sessions.

pages.body:
Profile contains settings that belong to the user rather than to a studio.

## MVP Sections

- [[Profile Personal Data]] for name, avatar, language and timezone.
- [[Profile Sessions]] for active session management.

Studio-level settings remain in [[Studio Settings]].

pages.title: Game Portfolio Insights
pages.description: Portfolio-level status for games in the studio.

frontmatter.title: Game Portfolio Insights
frontmatter.description: Portfolio-level status for games in the studio.

pages.body:
Game Portfolio Insights summarizes the studio's games.

## MVP Examples

- Games by publishing status.
- Games waiting for required promo assets.
- Games with recent RGS or publishing check results.
- Live games.

Detailed game-level metrics belong in [[Game Insights]].

pages.title: Team Insights
pages.description: Team-level activity overview for the studio.

frontmatter.title: Team Insights
frontmatter.description: Team-level activity overview for the studio.

pages.body:
Team Insights helps owners understand collaboration across the studio.

## MVP Examples

- Active members.
- Recent participation by game.
- Access distribution across the team.
- Review and publishing participation where available.

The page should avoid internal audit details in external documentation.

pages.title: Studio Insights
pages.description: MVP analytics for team activity and game portfolio status.

frontmatter.title: Studio Insights
frontmatter.description: MVP analytics for team activity and game portfolio status.

pages.body:
Studio Insights gives the studio a lightweight overview of work across the team and game portfolio.

## MVP Sections

- [[Insights Team]] summarizes team activity and participation.
- [[Insights Games]] summarizes game portfolio status and publishing progress.

Insights in MVP should support operational awareness. Financial analytics and advanced reporting are outside this external MVP surface.

pages.description: Studio settings for member access and invite defaults.

frontmatter.title: Members Settings
frontmatter.description: Studio settings for member access and invite defaults.

pages.body:
Members Settings is the administration surface for team access.

## MVP Actions

- View current studio members.
- Invite new members through [[Members Invite]].
- Apply [[Members Role Templates]].
- Edit [[Members Permissions]].
- Review [[Members Game Access]].
- Remove members through [[Members Remove]].

Permission changes should be understandable before they are saved because they can affect access to publishing and game tools.

frontmatter.title: Studio Settings

pages.body:
Studio Settings contains the configuration that is needed for the MVP workflow.

## MVP Sections

- [[Settings Basics]] for studio identity.
- [[Studio Settings Members]] for team access.
- [[Settings AI Wizard]] for assistant preferences.
- [[Settings My Profile]] as a shortcut to personal profile settings.

pages.title: Studio
pages.description: The organization workspace for team access, game portfolio and MVP publishing operations.

frontmatter.title: Studio
frontmatter.description: The organization workspace for team access, game portfolio and MVP publishing operations.

pages.body:
A Studio is the organization-level workspace in Playtagon. It contains the team, permissions, game portfolio, profile access and MVP-level insights.

## What a Studio Does in MVP

- Gives approved users access to the platform through [[Auth]].
- Shows the studio game portfolio in [[Games]].
- Lets owners manage team access through [[Studio Settings Members]].
- Stores basic studio identity in [[Settings Basics]].
- Provides MVP analytics through [[Studio Insights]].

## Main Navigation

- [[Games]] is the default working area after sign-in.
- [[Studio Settings]] contains studio basics, members, AI Wizard settings and profile entry points.
- [[Profile]] is the user's personal account area.

headings.text: What a Studio Does in MVP

pages.title: Game AI Wizard
pages.description: Contextual AI help inside one game workspace.

frontmatter.title: Game AI Wizard
frontmatter.description: Contextual AI help inside one game workspace.

pages.body:
Game AI Wizard is the game-scoped version of [[AI Wizard]]. It uses the active game context to help with MVP tasks.

## MVP Help Areas

- Explain SDK and RGS concepts.
- Help interpret archive or publishing check results.
- Suggest next steps for missing promo assets.
- Guide the team through publishing fields and review comments.

The Wizard should not imply that future tools are currently available.

pages.title: Game Documentation
pages.description: In-product help connected to the game workspace.

frontmatter.title: Game Documentation
frontmatter.description: In-product help connected to the game workspace.

pages.body:
Game Documentation is the help surface available from a game workspace.

## MVP Content

- Play SDK basics and `.playtagon` archive expectations.
- Resources upload guidance.
- Launch Game testing guidance.
- Promo Assets requirements.
- Publishing submission guidance.

For technical protocol details, use the SDK and RGS documentation in the dedicated folders.

pages.title: Game Insights
pages.description: Game-level readiness and live status signals.

frontmatter.title: Game Insights
frontmatter.description: Game-level readiness and live status signals.

pages.body:
Game Insights gives a focused view of one game's status.

## MVP Signals

- Current publishing status.
- Latest RGS or publishing check result.
- Promo asset readiness.
- Live status when the game has been deployed.

Advanced analytics can evolve later; MVP insights should help the team understand whether the game can move forward.

Game Members controls who can access a specific game.

## MVP Actions

- Invite a new collaborator through [[Members Invite]].

pages.description: Settings for one game workspace.

frontmatter.title: Game Settings
frontmatter.description: Settings for one game workspace.

pages.body:
Game Settings stores configuration for a specific game.

## MVP Scope

- Internal game name used inside the studio.
- Game-level member access through [[Game Settings Members]].
- Basic workspace preferences.

The public title, slug and description are configured in [[Publishing Game Foundation]], not here.

pages.title: Game Tools
pages.description: MVP tools available inside a game workspace.

frontmatter.title: Game Tools
frontmatter.description: MVP tools available inside a game workspace.

pages.body:
Game Tools are the working modules inside a game.

## MVP Tools

| Tool | Purpose |
| --- | --- |
| [[Resources]] | Upload and manage the `.playtagon` archive and supporting files. |
| [[Launch Game]] | Test the archive through the RGS game iframe and Playtagon controls. |
| [[Promo Assets]] | Prepare required artwork and approved asset URLs. |
| [[Publishing]] | Submit the game for review and move an approved version toward live. |

Future design, localization, simulation and task-management tools are not part of this external MVP folder.

God Mode is a testing mode for controlled outcomes and edge cases when supported by the game and RGS.

- Reproduce edge cases.
- Validate UI and round handling.

God Mode is for testing only and does not define live game behavior.

pages.body:
Launch Game lets the team test a built archive through RGS before and during publishing.

## MVP Surface

- RGS iframe with the game canvas.
- Playtagon controls for balance, currency and scenarios.
- Replay and debug-oriented helpers where available.

Launch Game is a test stand. Live distribution happens only after [[Publishing]].

pages.description: Artwork workflow for the required publishing assets.

frontmatter.title: Promo Assets
frontmatter.description: Artwork workflow for the required publishing assets.

pages.body:
Promo Assets manages the public artwork required before a game can pass Game Foundation readiness.

## Required Asset Types

| Asset | Size |
| --- | --- |
| Small UI Icon | 40 x 40 |
| Software Card Preview | 300 x 188 |
| App Tile Icon | 300 x 300 |
| Large Product Icon | 500 x 500 |
| Game Promo Thumbnail | 780 x 1040 |

Each required type needs an approved default asset. [[Publishing Game Foundation]] checks this readiness automatically. RGS receives external URLs to approved assets, not raw files.

frontmatter.description: Important signals around review and live deployment.

Publishing Alerts notify the team about important publishing events.

## MVP Examples

- Submission moved to review.
- Review passed or failed.
- 48-hour period started.
- Deployment started.
- Game is live.

pages.body:
Game Foundation is the first publishing module.

## MVP Fields

- Public game title.
- Game slug.
- Game description.
- Automatic readiness check for required [[Promo Assets]].

If any required promo asset type lacks an approved default, Game Foundation remains incomplete.

pages.title: Publishing Lifecycle
pages.description: Status path from draft submission to live game.

frontmatter.title: Publishing Lifecycle
frontmatter.description: Status path from draft submission to live game.

pages.body:
The MVP publishing lifecycle uses clear statuses so the studio knows what happens next.

| Status | Meaning |
| --- | --- |
| Collect Info | The submission is being prepared. |
| On Review | The submission is being reviewed. |
| Review Pass / Review Fail | Review completed with a result. |
| 48h Period | Safety window before deployment. |
| Game Deployment | The approved version is being deployed. |
| Live | The game is live through RGS. |

The 48-hour period gives the studio time to cancel deployment before the game goes live.

pages.title: Publishing Math
pages.description: Archive version, developer confirmations and RGS auto-checks.

frontmatter.title: Publishing Math
frontmatter.description: Archive version, developer confirmations and RGS auto-checks.

pages.body:
Publishing Math is the second publishing module.

## MVP Inputs

- Selected `.playtagon` archive version from [[Resources Game Archive]].
- Developer self-checkers confirming responsibility for game math.
- RGS auto-checks such as archive integrity, manifest validation, RTP validation, round correctness and randomizer usage.

pages.body:
RGS, or Remote Game Server, executes game logic and math. Playtagon manages the studio interface, files, review workflow and approved asset references; RGS runs the game runtime and validation side.

## Where RGS Is Used

- Building a `.playtagon` archive uploaded through [[Resources]].
- Serving the game iframe used by [[Launch Game]].
- Running custom scenarios and replay rounds when supported.
- Returning validation and auto-check results for [[Publishing Math]].
- Receiving approved external asset URLs from [[Promo Assets]].

## MVP Boundary

In the MVP, RGS is visible to studios through archive status, launch testing and publishing checks. Internal runtime operations, infrastructure details and reviewer-only tooling should stay out of external documentation unless they are part of the public API contract.

- [[Types Play SDK|Play SDK]]: developer-facing creator type.
- [[Resources]]: archive upload and build status.
- [[Launch Game]]: iframe runtime.
- [[Publishing Math]]: RGS checks used during submission.
- [[RGS API Protocol]]: transport-level API contract.
- [[Reference|RGS API Reference]]: API method reference.

pages.title: SDK
pages.description: Playtagon SDK for developers: .playtagon archives, rounds, custom scenarios, AI-friendly docs and RGS integration.

frontmatter.title: SDK
frontmatter.description: Playtagon SDK for developers: .playtagon archives, rounds, custom scenarios, AI-friendly docs and RGS integration.

pages.body:
## Purpose

Playtagon SDK is the developer-facing integration path for studios that build game logic in code and connect it to Playtagon through RGS. In the MVP, this is the only active creator type.

The SDK path is designed for teams that build locally, package a game as a `.playtagon` archive, upload that archive to [[Resources]], test it in [[Launch Game]] and submit it through [[Publishing]].

## AI-Friendly SDK

The SDK documentation is written so both developers and AI assistants can use it reliably. [[AI Wizard]] can answer SDK and [[RGS]] questions directly inside Playtagon.

- Every integration step should be documented with endpoints, parameters and expected formats.
- API methods should state the permissions and context required to call them.
- Data structures should be described in a machine-readable way where possible.
- SDK documentation should be usable from the product, especially during archive upload, launch testing and publishing.

## Terminology: Rounds, Not Spins

The SDK uses neutral terminology because Playtagon can support more than slot-style games.

> **Round** is the base game unit across SDK, RGS, documentation and publishing checks.

Use `round` in external docs, API examples and support answers unless a specific game mechanic requires a narrower term.

## Onboarding Quiz

During first setup, a developer can answer a short onboarding quiz:

- RGS type: Playtagon RGS or third-party RGS.
- Game mechanics: base game, bonuses and supported modes.
- Target platforms and client expectations.

The answers can generate a starter configuration and point the developer to the relevant SDK sections.

## Integration Flow

1. Create or join a studio in Playtagon.
2. Create a game with the [[Types Play SDK|Play SDK creator type]].
3. Build and test game logic locally.
4. Package the game as a `.playtagon` archive.
5. Upload the archive in [[Resources]].
6. Wait for RGS build and validation status.
7. Test the game in [[Launch Game]].
8. Prepare [[Promo Assets]].
9. Submit the selected archive version through [[Publishing]].

Standalone math simulation is not part of the external MVP app flow. Required checks are handled through RGS and publishing.

## `.playtagon` Archive

Playtagon uses a branded archive format named `.playtagon`. This is the expected upload artifact for the Play SDK path.

```text
game.playtagon
├── manifest.json     # metadata, versions and RGS configuration
├── math/             # game math, rounds, RTP and mechanics
├── assets/           # static assets when needed
└── config/           # permissions and mechanics configuration
```

`manifest.json` is required. Without it, the archive cannot be accepted.

After upload, the archive appears in [[Resources]], where [[RGS]] builds it and returns status.

## CLI

```bash
# Sign in
playtagon login

# Upload a game archive
playtagon upload ./game.playtagon --studio my-studio --game my-game

# Check upload or validation status
playtagon status --game my-game
```

The CLI is useful for CI/CD pipelines when a team wants archive upload to be part of an automated build.

## Custom Scenarios

The SDK can expose custom scenarios for testing. A scenario is a predefined game event sequence, such as forcing a bonus trigger or a specific round outcome.

1. The developer defines scenarios in SDK code.
2. The uploaded archive makes those scenarios available to RGS.
3. [[Launch Game]] lets the developer choose a scenario when supported.
4. RGS executes the scenario and renders the result in the game iframe.

Scenario selection belongs to the launch runtime. It should be treated as a testing feature, not live game behavior.

## RGS Statistics

When an archive is available, Playtagon can request RGS-side information for the game workspace, such as:

- historical round data;
- payout data;
- technical metrics;
- build and validation state.

The external MVP docs should describe only the information visible to studios, not internal RGS operations.

## Related Pages

- [[RGS]]: runtime layer for game logic and validation.
- [[Resources]]: `.playtagon` archive upload.
- [[Launch Game]]: test stand and custom scenarios.
- [[Publishing]]: selected archive version goes through review.
- [[AI Wizard]]: SDK and RGS support inside Playtagon.

pages.title: RGS API Reference
pages.description: RGS API method reference: Upload, Common, Publishing, Admin Publishing and Simulation APIs, plus errors and field limits.

frontmatter.title: RGS API Reference
frontmatter.description: RGS API method reference: Upload, Common, Publishing, Admin Publishing and Simulation APIs, plus errors and field limits.

pages.body:
This is the developer-facing method reference for implementing an RGS client. The transport-level contract, authentication, response envelope, operations, webhooks and binary archive upload rules are described in [[RGS API Protocol]].

## Contents

1. [Overview](#1-overview)
2. [Environments and Credentials](#2-environments-and-credentials)
3. [Quickstart](#3-quickstart)
4. [Response Handling](#4-response-handling)
5. [Method Reference](#5-method-reference)
6. [Error Code Registry](#6-error-code-registry)
7. [Field Limits](#7-field-limits)

## 1. Overview

Playtagon communicates with RGS server-to-server and acts as an integrator for multiple game studios. Each Studio API request identifies the concrete studio through `studio_id`; Playtagon itself authenticates to RGS with one credential pair issued for the integrator.

| Path | Purpose | Auth |
| --- | --- | --- |
| `POST /playtagon/v1?method=<name>` | Studio actions: upload, launch, publishing, simulation and queries | Studio pair |
| `POST /playtagon/admin/v1?method=<name>` | Admin or reviewer actions | Admin pair |
| `POST /playtagon/v1/upload/archive?...` | Binary `.playtagon` archive upload | Studio pair |

Most methods are JSON in, JSON out. Archive upload uses a binary body.

## 2. Environments and Credentials

The RGS operator provides the base URL and two `(Id, Secret)` pairs out-of-band.

| Pair | Used for |
| --- | --- |
| Studio | `/playtagon/v1` and `/playtagon/v1/upload/archive` |
| Admin | `/playtagon/admin/v1` |

Every request includes:

```text
Id:   <Id>
Sign: md5(<data> + <Secret>)   # lowercase hex, 32 characters
```

For JSON methods, `<data>` is the exact request body bytes. For binary archive upload, `<data>` is the raw query string without the leading `?`.

See [[RGS API Protocol#2-authentication]] for full signing rules.

## 3. Quickstart

End-to-end flow: register a game ID, upload an archive and wait for the upload operation to finish.

### Step 1: Register `game_id`

`POST /playtagon/v1?method=upload.register_game_id`

```json
{ "studio_id": "studio_acme", "game_id": "slots_classic" }
```

Response:

```json
{ "code": 0 }
```

### Step 2: Upload Archive

`POST /playtagon/v1/upload/archive`

```text
?studio_id=studio_acme
&game_id=slots_classic
&size=10485760
&sha256=<64_hex_chars>
&model_version=1.0
&client_version=1.0
```

Body: raw `.playtagon` archive bytes.

Response:

```json
{ "code": 0, "operation_id": "3f4a2b7c-1d8e-4e5f-b9a0-123456789abc" }
```

### Step 3: Wait for Completion

Poll `upload.get_operation_status` or subscribe to webhooks.

`POST /playtagon/v1?method=upload.get_operation_status`

```json
{
  "studio_id": "studio_acme",
  "operation_id": "3f4a2b7c-1d8e-4e5f-b9a0-123456789abc"
}
```

Response:

```json
{
  "code": 0,
  "operation_id": "3f4a2b7c-1d8e-4e5f-b9a0-123456789abc",
  "status": "done",
  "sub_operations": [
    { "name": "unpack_archive",     "status": "done" },
    { "name": "validate_structure", "status": "done" },
    { "name": "run_autotests",      "status": "done" }
  ]
}
```

## 4. Response Handling

Domain responses always use HTTP `200`.

Success:

```json
{ "code": 0, "...": "method-specific fields" }
```

Error:

```json
{ "code": 1042, "error": "Human-readable message" }
```

`code == 0` means success. Any non-zero `code` is a domain error.

Structural validation failures use `100 INCORRECT_REQUEST_PARAMETERS` and name the parameter in `error`. Method-specific errors are listed in [Error Code Registry](#6-error-code-registry).

## 5. Method Reference

Method groups:

- `upload.*`
- `common.*`
- `publishing.*`
- `admin.publishing.*`
- `simulation.*`

Long-running flows share one pattern:

- a creator method returns `operation_id`;
- `*.get_operation_status` returns a snapshot;
- `*.get_last_operation_id(studio_id, game_id)` helps recover after network failures;
- `*.cancel_operation(operation_id)` cancels active work when allowed.

See [[RGS API Protocol#7-operations-and-sub-operations]] and [[RGS API Protocol#8-webhooks]].

## 5.1 Upload API

Upload API registers games and uploads `.playtagon` archives.

### `upload.check_game_id`

Checks whether a `game_id` is available.

```text
POST /playtagon/v1?method=upload.check_game_id
```

Request:

```json
{ "studio_id": "studio_42", "game_id": "slots_classic" }
```

Response:

```json
{ "code": 0, "is_free": true }
```

Errors: `100`.

### `upload.register_game_id`

Registers `game_id` for a studio. Idempotent for the same studio and globally unavailable for other studios after registration.

```text
POST /playtagon/v1?method=upload.register_game_id
```

Request:

```json
{ "studio_id": "studio_42", "game_id": "slots_classic" }
```

Response:

```json
{ "code": 0 }
```

Errors: `1001 GAME_ID_ALREADY_TAKEN`.

### `upload.upload_archive`

Uploads a `.playtagon` archive and returns an upload `operation_id`. This method uses the binary upload endpoint, not JSON.

```text
POST /playtagon/v1/upload/archive
  ?studio_id=studio_42
  &game_id=slots_classic
  &size=10485760
  &sha256=<64_hex_chars>
  &model_version=1.0
  &client_version=1.0
  [&webhook_url=https://...&webhook_secret=<secret>]
```

At least one of `model_version` or `client_version` is required.

Response:

```json
{ "code": 0, "operation_id": "3f4a2b7c-1d8e-4e5f-b9a0-123456789abc" }
```

Errors: `1011`, `1012`, `1013`, `1014`, `1015`, `1016`, `1017`.

### `upload.get_last_operation_id`

Returns the latest upload operation for a game. Used for recovery after timeouts.

```text
POST /playtagon/v1?method=upload.get_last_operation_id
```

Request:

```json
{ "studio_id": "studio_42", "game_id": "slots_classic" }
```

Response:

```json
{ "code": 0, "operation_id": "3f4a2b7c-1d8e-4e5f-b9a0-123456789abc" }
```

`operation_id` is `null` if no upload operation exists for the game.

Errors: `1011`.

### `upload.get_operation_status`

Returns an upload operation snapshot.

```text
POST /playtagon/v1?method=upload.get_operation_status
```

Request:

```json
{
  "studio_id": "studio_42",
  "operation_id": "3f4a2b7c-1d8e-4e5f-b9a0-123456789abc"
}
```

Response:

```json
{
  "code": 0,
  "operation_id": "3f4a2b7c-1d8e-4e5f-b9a0-123456789abc",
  "status": "in_process",
  "sub_operations": [
    { "name": "unpack_archive",     "status": "done" },
    { "name": "validate_structure", "status": "in_process" },
    { "name": "run_autotests",      "status": "not_started" }
  ]
}
```

Errors: `1051`.

### `upload.cancel_operation`

Cancels an active upload operation. Canceling an already canceled operation is a success no-op.

```text
POST /playtagon/v1?method=upload.cancel_operation
```

Request:

```json
{
  "studio_id": "studio_42",
  "operation_id": "3f4a2b7c-1d8e-4e5f-b9a0-123456789abc"
}
```

Response:

```json
{ "code": 0 }
```

Errors: `1051`, `1071`.

## 5.2 Common API

Common API exposes read-only game state produced by upload and publishing flows.

### `common.list_games`

Lists all `game_id` values owned by the studio.

```text
POST /playtagon/v1?method=common.list_games
```

Request:

```json
{ "studio_id": "studio_42" }
```

Response:

```json
{ "code": 0, "games": ["slots_classic", "wild_west", "deep_sea"] }
```

Errors: `100`.

### `common.list_versions`

Lists uploaded model and client versions for a game.

```text
POST /playtagon/v1?method=common.list_versions
```

Request:

```json
{ "studio_id": "studio_42", "game_id": "slots_classic" }
```

Response:

```json
{
  "code": 0,
  "model_versions":  ["1.0", "1.1", "2.0"],
  "client_versions": ["1.0", "1.1"]
}
```

Errors: `2001`.

### `common.get_launch_url`

Returns a launch URL for a concrete version pair. If one or both versions are omitted, RGS resolves the latest matching archive.

```text
POST /playtagon/v1?method=common.get_launch_url
```

Request:

```json
{
  "studio_id": "studio_42",
  "game_id": "slots_classic",
  "model_version": "1.1",
  "client_version": "1.0"
}
```

`model_version` and `client_version` are optional.

Response:

```json
{
  "code": 0,
  "url": "https://rgs.example.com/launch/abc123",
  "model_version": "1.1",
  "client_version": "1.0"
}
```

Errors: `2001`, `2021`.

### `common.list_replays`

Returns a paginated list of rounds for `(game_id, user_id)`, sorted by timestamp descending. Money amounts are integers in denomination units.

```text
POST /playtagon/v1?method=common.list_replays
```

Request:

```json
{
  "studio_id": "studio_42",
  "game_id": "slots_classic",
  "user_id": "u_8842",
  "limit": 20,
  "offset": 0
}
```

Response:

```json
{
  "code": 0,
  "total": 137,
  "limit": 20,
  "offset": 0,
  "replays": [
    {
      "round_id":        "9b1d3c2e-7a4f-4c8b-b2e1-f5a6c7d8e9f0",
      "timestamp":       "2026-04-17T12:34:56Z",
      "bet_amount":      100,
      "win_amount":      250,
      "currency":        "USD",
      "denomination":    100,
      "client_ver":      "1.0",
      "server_ver":      "1.1",
      "game_session_id": "sess_aef812"
    }
  ]
}
```

Errors: `2001`.

### `common.get_replay`

Returns a replay viewer URL for one round.

```text
POST /playtagon/v1?method=common.get_replay
```

Request:

```json
{
  "studio_id": "studio_42",
  "round_id": "9b1d3c2e-7a4f-4c8b-b2e1-f5a6c7d8e9f0"
}
```

Response:

```json
{ "code": 0, "url": "https://rgs.example.com/replay/9b1d3c2e..." }
```

Errors: `2061`.

### `common.get_publishing_status`

Returns whether the game is published and which versions are live.

```text
POST /playtagon/v1?method=common.get_publishing_status
```

Request:

```json
{ "studio_id": "studio_42", "game_id": "slots_classic" }
```

Published response:

```json
{
  "code": 0,
  "is_published": true,
  "published": {
    "model_version":  "1.1",
    "client_version": "1.0",
    "published_at":   "2026-04-15T09:00:00Z"
  }
}
```

Unpublished response:

```json
{ "code": 0, "is_published": false }
```

When `is_published` is `false`, `published` is absent.

Errors: `2001`.

## 5.3 Publishing API

Publishing API submits a game version for automated checks, manual review and catalog publication.

### `publishing.submit`

Starts publishing for a concrete model/client version pair.

```text
POST /playtagon/v1?method=publishing.submit
```

Request:

```json
{
  "studio_id":         "studio_42",
  "game_id":           "slots_classic",
  "game_name":         "My Slot",
  "model_version":     "1.0",
  "client_version":    "1.0",
  "languages":         ["en", "es"],
  "supports_social":   true,
  "supports_gambling": true,
  "thumbnails": {
    "icon40x40":   "data:image/png;base64,...",
    "icon300x188": "data:image/png;base64,...",
    "icon300x300": "data:image/png;base64,...",
    "icon500x500": "data:image/png;base64,..."
  },
  "webhook_url":    "https://hooks.example.com/playtagon",
  "webhook_secret": "<32..256 chars>"
}
```

Rules:

- `languages` uses ISO 639-1 two-letter codes.
- All four thumbnail slots are required.
- Thumbnails must be valid PNG files with exact expected dimensions and <= 1 MB after base64 decoding.
- `webhook_url` and `webhook_secret` are optional but recommended.

Response:

```json
{ "code": 0, "operation_id": "3f4a2b7c-1d8e-4e5f-b9a0-123456789abc" }
```

Errors: `3001`, `3002`, `3003`, `3004`.

### `publishing.get_last_operation_id`

Returns the latest publishing operation for a game.

```text
POST /playtagon/v1?method=publishing.get_last_operation_id
```

Request:

```json
{ "studio_id": "studio_42", "game_id": "slots_classic" }
```

Response:

```json
{ "code": 0, "operation_id": "3f4a2b7c-1d8e-4e5f-b9a0-123456789abc" }
```

`operation_id` is `null` if there has been no publishing operation for the game.

Errors: `3001`.

### `publishing.get_operation_status`

Returns a publishing operation snapshot. Publishing sub-operation `data` appears only on failed sub-operations and contains `{ "error": "..." }`.

```text
POST /playtagon/v1?method=publishing.get_operation_status
```

Request:

```json
{
  "studio_id": "studio_42",
  "operation_id": "3f4a2b7c-1d8e-4e5f-b9a0-123456789abc"
}
```

Response:

```json
{
  "code": 0,
  "operation_id": "3f4a2b7c-1d8e-4e5f-b9a0-123456789abc",
  "status": "in_process",
  "sub_operations": [
    { "name": "autotest_payout",    "status": "done" },
    { "name": "autotest_assets",    "status": "done" },
    { "name": "manual_review",      "status": "in_process" },
    { "name": "publish_to_catalog", "status": "not_started" }
  ]
}
```

Errors: `3051`.

### `publishing.cancel_operation`

Cancels publishing before manual review starts. After a reviewer starts manual review, cancellation is blocked.

```text
POST /playtagon/v1?method=publishing.cancel_operation
```

Request:

```json
{
  "studio_id": "studio_42",
  "operation_id": "3f4a2b7c-1d8e-4e5f-b9a0-123456789abc"
}
```

Response:

```json
{ "code": 0 }
```

Errors: `3051`, `3071`, `3072`.

## 5.4 Admin Publishing API

Admin Publishing API is used by reviewers. It requires Admin credentials. Studio credentials are rejected. `studio_id` is not sent; operations are identified by `operation_id`.

Endpoint:

```text
POST /playtagon/admin/v1?method=admin.publishing.<name>
```

### `admin.publishing.list_for_review`

Lists operations waiting for manual review. Results are sorted oldest first.

Request:

```json
{}
```

Response:

```json
{
  "code": 0,
  "items": [
    {
      "studio_id":      "studio_42",
      "game_id":        "slots_classic",
      "game_name":      "My Slot",
      "model_version":  "1.0",
      "client_version": "1.0",
      "operation_id":   "3f4a2b7c-1d8e-4e5f-b9a0-123456789abc",
      "submitted_at":   "2026-04-17T12:34:56Z",
      "thumbnails": {
        "icon40x40":   "https://rgs.example.com/thumb/40x40/...",
        "icon300x188": "https://rgs.example.com/thumb/300x188/...",
        "icon300x300": "https://rgs.example.com/thumb/300x300/...",
        "icon500x500": "https://rgs.example.com/thumb/500x500/..."
      }
    }
  ]
}
```

Errors: `100`.

### `admin.publishing.list_in_autotests`

Lists operations whose automated checks are still running. Response shape matches `list_for_review`.

Errors: `100`.

### `admin.publishing.get_launch_url`

Returns a launch URL for reviewer testing.

Request:

```json
{ "operation_id": "3f4a2b7c-1d8e-4e5f-b9a0-123456789abc" }
```

Response:

```json
{
  "code": 0,
  "url":            "https://rgs.example.com/launch/abc123",
  "studio_id":      "studio_42",
  "game_id":        "slots_classic",
  "model_version":  "1.0",
  "client_version": "1.0"
}
```

Errors: `4041`.

### `admin.publishing.get_autotest_results`

Returns completed autotest results.

Request:

```json
{ "operation_id": "3f4a2b7c-1d8e-4e5f-b9a0-123456789abc" }
```

Response:

```json
{
  "code": 0,
  "autotest_results": {
    "autotest_payout": { "rtp": 96.4, "rounds": 1000000 },
    "autotest_assets": { "icons_ok": true, "manifest_ok": true }
  }
}
```

Errors: `4041`.

### `admin.publishing.start_review`

Moves `manual_review` from `not_started` to `in_process`.

Request:

```json
{ "operation_id": "3f4a2b7c-1d8e-4e5f-b9a0-123456789abc" }
```

Response:

```json
{ "code": 0 }
```

Errors: `4041`, `4081`, `4082`.

### `admin.publishing.confirm_review`

Approves manual review. `manual_review` becomes `done`, `publish_to_catalog` starts and the operation eventually becomes `done`.

Request:

```json
{ "operation_id": "3f4a2b7c-1d8e-4e5f-b9a0-123456789abc" }
```

Response:

```json
{ "code": 0 }
```

Errors: `4041`, `4101`.

### `admin.publishing.reject_review`

Rejects manual review with a reason. `manual_review` becomes `fail` and the top-level operation becomes `fail`.

Request:

```json
{
  "operation_id": "3f4a2b7c-1d8e-4e5f-b9a0-123456789abc",
  "reason": "forbidden imagery"
}
```

Response:

```json
{ "code": 0 }
```

Errors: `4041`, `4101`.

### `admin.publishing.download_archive`

Returns a link to the original archive uploaded by the studio. Works in any operation state.

Request:

```json
{ "operation_id": "3f4a2b7c-1d8e-4e5f-b9a0-123456789abc" }
```

Response:

```json
{
  "code": 0,
  "url":      "https://rgs.example.com/archives/abc123.playtagon",
  "filename": "slots_classic_1.0_1.0.playtagon",
  "size":     10485760,
  "sha256":   "<64_hex_chars>"
}
```

Errors: `4041`.

## 5.5 Simulation API

Simulation API runs long model-version calculations. In the external MVP app flow, standalone simulation is not exposed as a separate user tool, but the API group is documented for RGS integrations.

### `simulation.start`

Starts simulation for a model version.

```text
POST /playtagon/v1?method=simulation.start
```

Request:

```json
{
  "studio_id":      "studio_42",
  "game_id":        "slots_classic",
  "model_version":  "2.0",
  "webhook_url":    "https://hooks.example.com/playtagon",
  "webhook_secret": "<32..256 chars>"
}
```

`client_version` is not accepted. Simulation runs against `model_version`.

Response:

```json
{ "code": 0, "operation_id": "3f4a2b7c-1d8e-4e5f-b9a0-123456789abc" }
```

Errors: `5001`, `5002`, `5003`.

### `simulation.get_last_operation_id`

Returns the latest simulation operation for a game.

Request:

```json
{ "studio_id": "studio_42", "game_id": "slots_classic" }
```

Response:

```json
{ "code": 0, "operation_id": "3f4a2b7c-1d8e-4e5f-b9a0-123456789abc" }
```

Errors: `5001`.

### `simulation.get_operation_status`

Returns simulation status. Unlike upload and publishing, simulation sub-operations can include progress while `in_process` and result data when `done`.

Request:

```json
{
  "studio_id": "studio_42",
  "operation_id": "3f4a2b7c-1d8e-4e5f-b9a0-123456789abc"
}
```

Response:

```json
{
  "code": 0,
  "operation_id": "3f4a2b7c-1d8e-4e5f-b9a0-123456789abc",
  "status": "in_process",
  "sub_operations": [
    { "name": "rtp_calculation",       "status": "done",       "data": { "rtp": 96.43, "rounds": 1000000 } },
    { "name": "volatility_analysis",   "status": "in_process", "data": { "progress": 42 } },
    { "name": "hit_rate_distribution", "status": "not_started" }
  ]
}
```

Errors: `5051`.

### `simulation.cancel_operation`

Cancels an active simulation operation.

Request:

```json
{
  "studio_id": "studio_42",
  "operation_id": "3f4a2b7c-1d8e-4e5f-b9a0-123456789abc"
}
```

Response:

```json
{ "code": 0 }
```

Errors: `5051`, `5071`.

## 6. Error Code Registry

| Code | Slug | Meaning |
| --- | --- | --- |
| `100` | `INCORRECT_REQUEST_PARAMETERS` | Structural parameter validation failed. Returned by any method. |
| `1001` | `GAME_ID_ALREADY_TAKEN` | `game_id` is already registered by another studio. |
| `1011` | `UPLOAD_GAME_ID_NOT_OWNED` | `game_id` does not belong to the requesting `studio_id`. |
| `1012` | `UPLOAD_VERSIONS_ALREADY_EXIST` | `(model_version, client_version)` already exists for the game. |
| `1013` | `UPLOAD_ALREADY_IN_PROGRESS` | An active upload operation already exists for the game. |
| `1014` | `UPLOAD_SIZE_EXCEEDS_LIMIT` | Declared archive size exceeds the server limit. |
| `1015` | `UPLOAD_SIZE_MISMATCH` | Declared size and received byte count do not match. |
| `1016` | `UPLOAD_SHA256_MISMATCH` | Declared SHA256 does not match the received archive. |
| `1017` | `UPLOAD_STORAGE_ERROR` | Storage or staging write failed. |
| `1051` | `UPLOAD_OPERATION_NOT_FOUND` | Upload `operation_id` does not exist or belongs to another studio. |
| `1071` | `UPLOAD_OPERATION_ALREADY_FINISHED` | Tried to cancel terminal upload operation. |
| `2001` | `COMMON_GAME_ID_NOT_OWNED` | `game_id` does not belong to this `studio_id`. |
| `2021` | `COMMON_VERSION_NOT_FOUND` | Requested model/client version was not found. |
| `2061` | `COMMON_REPLAY_NOT_FOUND` | `round_id` does not exist or is not accessible. |
| `3001` | `PUBLISHING_GAME_ID_NOT_OWNED` | `game_id` does not belong to this `studio_id`. |
| `3002` | `PUBLISHING_VERSIONS_NOT_FOUND` | No successful archive upload exists for the requested version pair. |
| `3003` | `PUBLISHING_ALREADY_IN_PROGRESS` | Active publishing operation already exists for the game. |
| `3004` | `PUBLISHING_INVALID_THUMBNAIL` | Thumbnail is invalid, wrong size or too large. |
| `3051` | `PUBLISHING_OPERATION_NOT_FOUND` | Publishing `operation_id` does not exist or belongs to another studio. |
| `3071` | `PUBLISHING_OPERATION_ALREADY_FINISHED` | Tried to cancel terminal publishing operation. |
| `3072` | `PUBLISHING_CANNOT_CANCEL_IN_REVIEW` | Manual review has started; studio cancellation is blocked. |
| `4041` | `ADMIN_PUBLISHING_OPERATION_NOT_FOUND` | Admin publishing operation does not exist. |
| `4081` | `ADMIN_PUBLISHING_NOT_READY_FOR_REVIEW` | Operation is not ready for manual review. |
| `4082` | `ADMIN_PUBLISHING_ALREADY_IN_REVIEW` | Manual review is already in progress. |
| `4101` | `ADMIN_PUBLISHING_NOT_IN_REVIEW` | Manual review is not currently in progress. |
| `5001` | `SIMULATION_GAME_ID_NOT_OWNED` | `game_id` does not belong to this `studio_id`. |
| `5002` | `SIMULATION_MODEL_VERSION_NOT_FOUND` | Requested model version was not found. |
| `5003` | `SIMULATION_ALREADY_IN_PROGRESS` | Active simulation already exists for the game. |
| `5051` | `SIMULATION_OPERATION_NOT_FOUND` | Simulation `operation_id` does not exist or belongs to another studio. |
| `5071` | `SIMULATION_OPERATION_ALREADY_FINISHED` | Tried to cancel terminal simulation operation. |

Ranges: `100..999` common, `1000..1999` Upload, `2000..2999` Common, `3000..3999` Publishing, `4000..4999` Admin Publishing, `5000..5999` Simulation.

## 7. Field Limits

| Field | Limit |
| --- | --- |
| `studio_id` | 1..64, `^[a-zA-Z0-9_-]+$` |
| `game_id` | 1..32, `^[a-zA-Z0-9_-]+$`, case-insensitive |
| `user_id` | 1..36, opaque string |
| `model_version` / `client_version` | 1..32, `^[a-zA-Z0-9._-]+$` |
| `game_name` | 1..255 |
| `languages` | 1..50 items, each ISO 639-1 |
| `thumbnail` | PNG with exact required dimensions, <= 1 MB after base64 decoding |
| `reason` | 1..1000 |
| `webhook_url` | absolute URL, <= 2048 |
| `webhook_secret` | 32..256 |
| `sha256` | exactly 64 hex characters |
| `operation_id`, `round_id` | UUID v4, 36 characters with hyphens |

Archive `size` limit is configured by RGS. If the archive is too large, RGS returns `1014 UPLOAD_SIZE_EXCEEDS_LIMIT`.

## Related Pages

- [[RGS API Protocol]]: transport-level contract.
- [[SDK]]: developer-facing integration overview.
- [[RGS]]: runtime overview.

frontmatter.title: RGS API Protocol
frontmatter.description: Transport-level RGS API contract: requests, authentication, response envelope, operations, webhooks and binary archive upload.

pages.body:
This document describes how RGS API requests and responses work at the network level: what the client sends, what the server returns, how errors are represented and how long-running operations are tracked.

It is self-contained for client implementation. The method-by-method API reference is in [[Reference|RGS API Reference]].

## Contents

1. [Request Format](#1-request-format)
2. [Authentication](#2-authentication)
3. [Response Envelope](#3-response-envelope)
4. [Error Codes](#4-error-codes)
5. [`studio_id`](#5-studio_id)
6. [Data Format Conventions](#6-data-format-conventions)
7. [Operations and Sub-Operations](#7-operations-and-sub-operations)
8. [Webhooks](#8-webhooks)
9. [Binary Archive Upload](#9-binary-archive-upload)

## 1. Request Format

The API uses three base paths.

| Path | Purpose |
| --- | --- |
| `POST /playtagon/v1?method=<name>` | Studio API: actions performed on behalf of studios. |
| `POST /playtagon/admin/v1?method=<name>` | Admin API: reviewer/admin actions. |
| `POST /playtagon/v1/upload/archive?...` | Binary game archive upload. See [Binary Archive Upload](#9-binary-archive-upload). |

For JSON methods:

- **HTTP method:** always `POST`.
- **URL:** method name in the `method` query parameter, for example `?method=upload.register_game_id`.
- **Method names:** dot-separated, `<group>.<action>`.
- **Content-Type:** `application/json; charset=utf-8`.
- **Body:** JSON object with method parameters. Empty `{}` is allowed for methods without parameters.
- **Versioning:** incompatible changes require a new path prefix such as `/v2`.

Minimal request example:

```http
POST /playtagon/v1?method=upload.check_game_id HTTP/1.1
Host: rgs.example.com
Content-Type: application/json; charset=utf-8
Id: playtagon
Sign: 6a3e8b1c2f4d5e7f8a9b0c1d2e3f4a5b

{"studio_id":"studio_42","game_id":"slots_classic"}
```

## 2. Authentication

Each request sends two headers.

| Header | Value |
| --- | --- |
| `Id` | Sender identifier issued by the RGS operator. |
| `Sign` | Request signature: `md5(<data> + <Secret>)`, lowercase 32-character hex. |

### Calculating `Sign`

- For JSON methods, `<data>` is the exact request body bytes sent over the wire. Any re-serialization can break the signature, including whitespace changes, key ordering or Unicode escaping.
- For binary archive upload, `<data>` is the raw query string without the leading `?`, exactly as it appears in the URL.

```text
Sign = lowercase_hex(md5(<data> + <Secret>))
```

Example: for body `{"studio_id":"studio_42","game_id":"slots_classic"}` and secret `change-me-studio-secret`, sign the exact string `{"studio_id":"studio_42","game_id":"slots_classic"}change-me-studio-secret`.

### Credential Pairs

- **Studio pair `(Id, Secret)`** is used for `/playtagon/v1` and `/playtagon/v1/upload/archive`.
- **Admin pair `(Id, Secret)`** is used only for `/playtagon/admin/v1`.

Both pairs are issued by the RGS operator out-of-band.

The Studio pair identifies the Playtagon integrator. A specific studio is identified by `studio_id` in the request body or upload query.

### Scheme Properties

- The secret is never sent over the network.
- Any body or query mutation invalidates the signature.
- There is no timestamp or nonce in the signature. Replay protection is handled through TLS, idempotent method design and operation state.

### Transport Authentication Errors

- Missing `Id` or `Sign`: HTTP `401`, no JSON envelope.
- Unknown `Id` or signature mismatch: HTTP `403`, no JSON envelope.

These are infrastructure-level errors, not API domain errors.

## 3. Response Envelope

Every domain-level response is JSON with HTTP `200`, whether the call succeeded or failed at the domain level.

Success:

```json
{
  "code": 0,
  "...": "method-specific fields"
}
```

Domain error:

```json
{
  "code": 1042,
  "error": "Human-readable message"
}
```

Rules:

- `code == 0` means success. `error` is absent.
- `code != 0` means domain error. `error` is required.
- Extended error details are not part of the contract. Machines read `code`; people read `error`.

### Non-200 Responses

HTTP 4xx/5xx are reserved for infrastructure problems:

- `401` / `403`: authentication failed.
- `400`: request body is not valid JSON or not a JSON object.
- `413`: request body exceeds server or proxy limits.
- `5xx`: server failure.

Clients should distinguish these cases by HTTP status code.

## 4. Error Codes

The integer `code` identifies the domain error type. Each code has a fixed `SCREAMING_SNAKE_CASE` slug and a default human-readable message.

| Range | Group |
| --- | --- |
| `100..999` | Common validation and cross-cutting errors |
| `1000..1999` | Upload API |
| `2000..2999` | Common API |
| `3000..3999` | Publishing API |
| `4000..4999` | Admin Publishing API |
| `5000..5999` | Simulation API |

Each method group reserves space for future codes.

### Validation Error `100`

Any structural validation failure returns `100 INCORRECT_REQUEST_PARAMETERS`. The `error` field must name the specific parameter.

```json
{ "code": 100, "error": "Not found game_id" }
{ "code": 100, "error": "Incorrect parameter type for size: expected integer" }
{ "code": 100, "error": "Incorrect parameter range for size: between 1 and 700000000" }
{ "code": 100, "error": "Incorrect parameter format for game_id: expected ^[a-zA-Z0-9_-]+$" }
```

Structural validation happens before domain validation. If parameter format, length or range fails, the server does not check entity existence, ownership or state.

All non-`100` codes are method-specific domain errors. See [[Reference#6-error-code-registry|RGS API Reference]].

## 5. `studio_id`

Every request to `/playtagon/v1` must include `studio_id`.

```json
{ "studio_id": "<string>", "...": "method parameters" }
```

Rules:

- Format: `^[a-zA-Z0-9_-]+$`.
- Length: 1..64.
- Structural violation returns `100 INCORRECT_REQUEST_PARAMETERS`.
- Admin API methods do not accept `studio_id`; they identify work through `operation_id` or method-specific parameters.

## 6. Data Format Conventions

### Field Names

Fields use `snake_case` on the wire. Clients may map them to another naming convention internally.

### Dates and Times

Server responses use UTC ISO-8601, for example `"2026-04-17T12:34:56Z"`.

### Identifiers

- `operation_id` and `round_id` are UUID v4 strings in canonical 36-character form.
- `studio_id`, `game_id` and `user_id` are strings with method-specific validation.
- `game_id` is case-insensitive for comparison and indexing. The server may return the value as registered.

### Money Amounts

Amounts are integers in denomination units. Currency and denomination are returned as separate fields.

## 7. Operations and Sub-Operations

Some API methods start long-running operations. The initial request creates the operation and returns `operation_id`; the client tracks progress separately.

Each operation:

- belongs to one flow: `upload`, `publishing` or `simulation`;
- has a top-level status: `in_process`, `done`, `fail` or `canceled`;
- contains a predefined ordered list of sub-operations;
- each sub-operation has status `not_started`, `in_process`, `done`, `fail` or `canceled`;
- each sub-operation may include an optional `data` object.

### `data` Rules

| Flow | When `data` appears | Content |
| --- | --- | --- |
| Upload / Publishing | only when a sub-operation is `fail` | `{ "error": "<text>" }` |
| Simulation | when a sub-operation is `in_process` | `{ "progress": <number 0..100> }` |
| Simulation | when a sub-operation is `fail` | `{ "error": "<text>" }` |
| Simulation | when a sub-operation is `done` | flow-specific result JSON |

`not_started` and `canceled` sub-operations do not include `data`.

### Status Snapshot

`*.get_operation_status(operation_id)` returns a snapshot:

```json
{
  "code": 0,
  "operation_id": "3f4a2b7c-1d8e-4e5f-b9a0-123456789abc",
  "status": "in_process",
  "sub_operations": [
    { "name": "unpack_archive",     "status": "done" },
    { "name": "validate_structure", "status": "in_process" },
    { "name": "run_autotests",      "status": "not_started" }
  ]
}
```

Failed sub-operation example:

```json
{ "name": "validate_structure", "status": "fail", "data": { "error": "missing manifest.json" } }
```

When `data` is not set, the field is absent rather than `null`.

### Concurrency

Only one active `in_process` operation may exist for the same `(studio_id, game_id)` inside one flow. Starting another operation before the previous one reaches `done`, `fail` or `canceled` returns the flow-specific `*_ALREADY_IN_PROGRESS` error.

### Network Recovery

Each flow has `*.get_last_operation_id(studio_id, game_id)`. If an operation-creating request times out, the client can call this method to discover whether the operation was created.

### Cancellation

Each flow has `*.cancel_operation(operation_id)`. Cancellation moves the operation to `canceled`.

| Previous sub-operation status | New status |
| --- | --- |
| `done` | `done` |
| `fail` | `fail` |
| `in_process` | `canceled` |
| `not_started` | `canceled` |

Cancellation does not add `data` to canceled sub-operations.

Idempotency:

- Canceling an already `canceled` operation returns `code: 0`.
- Canceling a terminal `done` or `fail` operation returns `*_OPERATION_ALREADY_FINISHED`.
- Publishing has one extra rule: once manual review has started, studio cancellation is blocked with `PUBLISHING_CANNOT_CANCEL_IN_REVIEW`.

## 8. Webhooks

RGS can send operation status events to the client instead of requiring polling.

### Subscription

Webhooks are optional and configured per operation. Operation-creating methods accept:

| Parameter | Type | Constraints |
| --- | --- | --- |
| `webhook_url` | string, optional | absolute `https://...` URL, length <= 2048 |
| `webhook_secret` | string, optional | length 32..256 |

Rules:

- Both parameters must be provided together or both omitted.
- If both are provided, RGS stores them with the operation and emits events for that operation.
- If both are absent, the client must poll `*.get_operation_status`.

The subscription exists only for the lifetime of the operation. Admin actions for a publishing operation emit to the same URL provided in `publishing.submit`.

### Server Request Format

```http
POST <webhook_url> HTTP/1.1
Content-Type: application/json; charset=utf-8
x-webhook-secret: <webhook_secret>

{
  "event_id":     "evt_abc123",
  "event_type":   "upload.status_changed",
  "timestamp":    "2026-04-17T12:34:56Z",
  "studio_id":    "<studio-id>",
  "operation_id": "3f4a2b7c-1d8e-4e5f-b9a0-123456789abc",
  "data":         { "...": "same shape as get_operation_status" }
}
```

- `event_id` is unique per event.
- `event_type` is `<flow>.status_changed`.
- `timestamp` is UTC ISO-8601.
- `data` is the operation snapshot returned by `*.get_operation_status`.

### Receiver Requirements

1. Verify `x-webhook-secret`.
2. Deduplicate by `event_id`; retries can deliver the same event more than once.
3. Return `2xx` after successful processing. Any other response is treated as delivery failure.

### Retries

Default retry behavior:

- 5 attempts.
- 10 seconds between attempts.
- 10 second request timeout.

After retries are exhausted, RGS stops delivering that event. Clients that need guaranteed final state should keep fallback polling.

Event order is not guaranteed across retries. Clients should process snapshots idempotently.

## 9. Binary Archive Upload

The archive upload endpoint is the only API endpoint with a non-JSON request body. It is used by `upload.upload_archive`.

### Endpoint

```text
POST /playtagon/v1/upload/archive?<parameters>
```

Metadata is sent in query parameters. The body contains raw `.playtagon` archive bytes.

```text
POST /playtagon/v1/upload/archive
  ?studio_id=<id>
  &game_id=<id>
  &size=<size_in_bytes>
  &sha256=<64_hex_chars>
  [&model_version=<version>]
  [&client_version=<version>]
  [&webhook_url=<url>&webhook_secret=<secret>]

Headers:
  Id:             <studio Id>
  Sign:           md5(<raw_query_string_without_leading_?> + <Secret>), hex lowercase
  Content-Type:   application/octet-stream
  Content-Length: <size>

Body:
  <raw archive bytes>
```

The signature is calculated from the raw query string, not the body. The query includes `size` and `sha256`; after receiving the body, the server verifies both.

### Required Parameters

| Parameter | Type | Description |
| --- | --- | --- |
| `studio_id` | string | See [`studio_id`](#5-studio_id). |
| `game_id` | string | Must be registered for this studio. |
| `size` | integer | Body size in bytes. |
| `sha256` | string | Archive SHA256 in 64-character hex. |

### Optional Parameters

| Parameter | Description |
| --- | --- |
| `model_version` | Game model version. |
| `client_version` | Game client version. |
| `webhook_url` | See [Webhooks](#8-webhooks). |
| `webhook_secret` | See [Webhooks](#8-webhooks). |

At least one of `model_version` or `client_version` is required.

### Server Validation Order

1. Authentication.
2. Structural validation of query parameters.
3. Domain pre-checks before reading the body: game ownership, version uniqueness, active upload operation and size limit.
4. If a pre-check fails, the server returns a JSON error and closes the connection.
5. Stream body to staging while counting bytes and calculating SHA256.
6. Verify actual byte count and SHA256 against the declared values.
7. Atomically move the file to final storage, create an upload operation and return `operation_id`.

### Response

```json
{ "code": 0, "operation_id": "3f4a2b7c-1d8e-4e5f-b9a0-123456789abc" }
```

Domain errors use the normal JSON envelope. Infrastructure errors can use non-200 HTTP status codes.

### Client Recommendations

- Use a realistic HTTP timeout. Large archives can take minutes on slow links.
- If upload transfer fails, call `upload.get_last_operation_id(studio_id, game_id)`.
- If an operation exists, continue tracking it.
- If no operation exists, retry upload from the beginning.
- Different games can upload in parallel. One game can have only one active upload operation at a time.

## Related Pages

- [[Reference|RGS API Reference]]: method-level API reference.
- [[SDK]] and [[RGS]]: developer-facing integration overview.

pages.body:
# Playtagon External Docs

Playtagon is a platform for building, testing, reviewing and publishing casino games. This external documentation covers the MVP path for studios and developers: create a game with the Play SDK, upload a `.playtagon` archive, test it through RGS, prepare promo assets and submit the game for publishing.

## MVP Scope

The external MVP documentation focuses on the active Play SDK workflow:

1. Join or create a studio.
2. Create a game with the Play SDK creator type.
3. Upload a `.playtagon` archive in [[Resources]].
4. Test the archive in [[Launch Game]].
5. Prepare required artwork in [[Promo Assets]].
6. Complete [[Publishing]] and move an approved version toward live.

Future creator types, visual game-building tools, standalone simulation tools, localization workflows and internal operations are intentionally outside this public MVP index.

## Documentation Map

| Section | Start here | What it covers |
| --- | --- | --- |
| [[Playtagon App]] | `10 Playtagon App/` | Studio workspace, game workspace, MVP tools and AI Wizard. |
| [[SDK]] | `60 SDK/` | Play SDK integration, `.playtagon` archives and RGS runtime overview. |
| [[RGS API Protocol]] | `70 RGS API Protocol/` | Transport-level RGS API contract: auth, response envelope, operations, webhooks and binary archive upload. |
| [[Reference]] | `70 RGS API Protocol/Reference.md` | RGS API method reference, error codes and field limits. |

## Core Concepts

**Studio** is the organization workspace where teams manage games, members and settings.

**Game** is the workspace for one Playtagon game, including Resources, Launch Game, Promo Assets and Publishing.

**Play SDK** is the active MVP creator type. Developers build locally and package the game as a `.playtagon` archive.

**RGS** is the Remote Game Server that builds archives, runs game logic, provides the launch iframe and returns validation checks.

**Publishing** is the review and deployment workflow that moves an approved game version toward live.

**AI Wizard** helps with onboarding, SDK/RGS questions, promo assets and publishing guidance.

## Internal Source

This repository is external-facing. Internal documentation may be used as source material, but public docs should be rewritten for an external audience and must not expose private implementation details, credentials, unreleased plans or internal operational procedures.
